briefing.headline: 10 high-priority items in the latest EM scan
briefing.summary: Top sources today: European Journal of Emergency Medicine, MedPage Today Emergency Medicine, REBEL EM. Main themes: trials, reviews, evidence synthesis. Best early reads: Association between patient-to-nurse ratios in emergency departments and patient, staff, and organizational outcomes: a systematic review; Whole Blood Not Better Than Components En Route to Hospital After Traumatic Injury.
briefing.editorialHeadline: Staffing pressure, PE escalation doubts, and a sharper CAP reset for the ED
briefing.editorialSummary:
Today's set is strongest where it forces everyday emergency care decisions back onto solid ground. The cleanest practice signal is the 2025 CAP update, which keeps pushing clinicians toward more deliberate imaging, more selective antibiotics, and shorter treatment when the syndrome and trajectory allow it. PRoMPT BOLUS does something similar for pediatric sepsis by removing one distraction: balanced fluid did not clearly beat saline, so the bedside emphasis stays on recognition, reassessment, vasoactive support, and source control rather than fluid tribalism.

The more operational papers are just as useful. A systematic review linking nurse workload to patient, staff, and organizational harm makes staffing feel less like a scheduling complaint and more like a safety variable. HI-PEITHO keeps intermediate-risk PE in the uncomfortable middle, where escalation options are expanding faster than certainty. Around that, the set reinforces several familiar but important themes: poisoned patients still need a toxidrome-first framework, ketamine safety still depends on disciplined setup, indeterminate troponins still need serial thinking, and geriatric prescribing remains an active ED risk. Overall, this is a pragmatic reading list about doing the basics better and being more honest about where evidence still does not justify overreach.

dailyEditorial.title: Association between patient-to-nurse ratios in emergency departments and patient, staff, and organizational outcomes: a systematic review
dailyEditorial.takeaway: Treat nurse ratios as a clinical quality variable, not just an operational annoyance. If your department is persistently stretched, expect downstream effects on delays, missed care, and staff attrition even when the evidence cannot give you a single universal safe number.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This review pushes a staffing problem into outcomes territory.
dailyEditorial.summary: This review pushes a staffing problem into outcomes territory. The signal across the literature is directionally consistent: heavier nurse loads in the ED track with worse patient flow, more strain on staff, and likely harm to safety, even if exact mortality effects remain hard to quantify because the underlying studies are heterogeneous.

dailyEditorial.title: Whole Blood Not Better Than Components En Route to Hospital After Traumatic Injury
dailyEditorial.takeaway: If your service already has whole blood, this does not make it wrong; it just stops short of proving superiority in the transport phase. Keep the focus on getting blood in early, minimizing delay, and delivering definitive hemorrhage control.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: TOWAR is useful mainly because it trims back some of the enthusiasm around prehospital whole blood.
dailyEditorial.summary: TOWAR is useful mainly because it trims back some of the enthusiasm around prehospital whole blood. In injured patients at risk of hemorrhagic shock, whole O blood did not clearly outperform component therapy on the outcomes that matter, which suggests logistics and availability may still matter more than product ideology in many systems.

dailyEditorial.title: HI-PEITHO Trial: Ultrasound-facilitated, Catheter Directed Fibrinolysis for PE
dailyEditorial.takeaway: For submassive or intermediate-risk PE, this is a discussion piece more than a bedside mandate. Involve PE response teams early when deterioration risk is real, but do not let catheter capability substitute for careful patient selection.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: HI-PEITHO keeps the intermediate-risk PE debate alive without settling it.
dailyEditorial.summary: HI-PEITHO keeps the intermediate-risk PE debate alive without settling it. Ultrasound-facilitated catheter-directed fibrinolysis may offer a route to escalation short of full-dose systemic lysis, but the practical question is still whether the extra procedure meaningfully improves patient-centered outcomes enough to justify complexity, bleeding exposure, and resource use.

dailyEditorial.title: Effect of a liquid crystal vein locator on intravenous catheterization in young children: a randomized trial
dailyEditorial.takeaway: If you work in a department that struggles with pediatric IV starts, this looks like a credible workflow upgrade rather than gadget creep. The value is highest where repeated attempts are common and staff are willing to standardize its use.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This pediatric trial is refreshingly practical: a simple vein locator improved first-pass IV success, cut repeat attempts, and shortened cannulation time in young children.
dailyEditorial.summary: This pediatric trial is refreshingly practical: a simple vein locator improved first-pass IV success, cut repeat attempts, and shortened cannulation time in young children. That matters because better vascular access is one of the few small process fixes that can immediately reduce distress for both children and staff.

dailyEditorial.title: ACMT Toxicology Visual Pearl – Sauté Gone Sour
dailyEditorial.takeaway: In a suspected deadly mushroom exposure, escalate early, involve toxicology, and do not be reassured by an initially mild presentation. Amanita-type poisonings can declare themselves late and badly.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This ALiEM toxicology pearl is brief but memorable: a destroying angel ingestion should make clinicians think first about delayed hepatotoxicity, not a benign GI upset or a quick hallucinogen story.
dailyEditorial.summary: This ALiEM toxicology pearl is brief but memorable: a destroying angel ingestion should make clinicians think first about delayed hepatotoxicity, not a benign GI upset or a quick hallucinogen story. Its value is educational rather than evidentiary, but the pattern recognition matters.

dailyEditorial.title: 2025 guideline updates for community-acquired pneumonia diagnosis and management
dailyEditorial.takeaway: Recheck your default CAP habits. Use imaging with purpose, avoid automatic antibiotics when the syndrome is credibly viral, and think in terms of minimum effective treatment rather than inherited longer-course routines.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: The 2025 CAP update is one of the most practice-relevant reads in the set because it supports narrower, shorter, and more deliberate treatment.
dailyEditorial.summary: The 2025 CAP update is one of the most practice-relevant reads in the set because it supports narrower, shorter, and more deliberate treatment. The useful shifts are better integration of point-of-care imaging, more selectivity around antibiotics when a viral diagnosis is convincing, and less attachment to reflexively long treatment courses.

dailyEditorial.title: PRoMPT BOLUS: A Landmark PECARN Trial Defining Fluid Choice in Pediatric Sepsis
dailyEditorial.takeaway: Either saline or balanced fluid is a defensible first-line choice in pediatric septic shock. What will matter more at the bedside is how quickly you recognize non-response and escalate beyond repeat bolus reflexes.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: PRoMPT BOLUS is important mostly because it narrows a controversy.
dailyEditorial.summary: PRoMPT BOLUS is important mostly because it narrows a controversy. In pediatric septic shock, balanced crystalloid did not deliver a major clinical advantage over saline, which is reassuring for pragmatic ED care and shifts the conversation back toward timing, reassessment, vasoactive support, and source control.

dailyEditorial.title: Management of patients with suspected but unidentified poisoning in the emergency department: a joint Royal College of Emergency Medicine and National Poisons Information Service best practice guideline
dailyEditorial.takeaway: When the agent is unclear, build the case from syndrome, timing, and trajectory, then get TOXBASE or poisons input early. The guideline is a scaffold for first-pass care, not a replacement for toxin-specific advice.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: The RCEM/NPIS guideline is strong because it gives clinicians a disciplined way to approach the poisoned patient before the toxin is known.
dailyEditorial.summary: The RCEM/NPIS guideline is strong because it gives clinicians a disciplined way to approach the poisoned patient before the toxin is known. It leans on toxidromes, toxicokinetics, and expected evolution rather than pretending an unidentified ingestion can be solved by a single generic algorithm.

dailyEditorial.title: Systematic Review of Pediatric Ketamine in Emergency Department Procedural Sedation: Frequency and Predictors of Adverse Events
dailyEditorial.takeaway: Ketamine is still a solid default for many pediatric procedural sedations, but the safety margin comes from setup and monitoring rather than from the drug’s reputation alone. Use the review to tighten counseling, staffing, and rescue readiness.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This systematic review is a good calibration piece for pediatric ketamine sedation.
dailyEditorial.summary: This systematic review is a good calibration piece for pediatric ketamine sedation. Ketamine remains a strong ED tool, but pooled adverse event data and predictor signals are a useful reminder that familiarity should not drift into complacency, especially around airway vigilance and preparation.

dailyEditorial.title: Minor Stroke, Major Uncertainty
dailyEditorial.takeaway: Do not let the label minor stroke make the decision for you. If disability is not clearly measurable and meaningful, routine IV thrombolysis becomes much harder to justify.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: The message here is restraint: minor stroke is not automatically benign, but neither is every mild deficit a thrombolysis win.
dailyEditorial.summary: The message here is restraint: minor stroke is not automatically benign, but neither is every mild deficit a thrombolysis win. The article argues for a more honest distinction between mild and truly non-disabling symptoms, which is where many real-world treatment debates live.

dailyEditorial.title: Medication Safety Strategies for Geriatric Patients in the Emergency Department: A Narrative Review
dailyEditorial.takeaway: In older patients, look deliberately for medication-caused illness and use the ED encounter to simplify therapy where you safely can. Reconciliation and deprescribing are clinical interventions, not administrative chores.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: Medication safety in older adults is framed here as core emergency medicine, not a ward clean-up exercise.
dailyEditorial.summary: Medication safety in older adults is framed here as core emergency medicine, not a ward clean-up exercise. The review is persuasive in showing how often polypharmacy, adverse effects, and prescribing cascades are part of the presenting problem rather than background noise.

dailyEditorial.title: TTL Tip 11: Be the TTL your prehospital teams want to hand over to
dailyEditorial.takeaway: If trauma handovers in your department feel chaotic, the fix is usually preparation and role clarity rather than more words. Build a repeatable script and make the receiving leader visibly ready before the patient arrives.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This trauma team leader tip is not new science, but it is useful operational craft.
dailyEditorial.summary: This trauma team leader tip is not new science, but it is useful operational craft. Good prehospital handover is less about polite listening than about preparing the room, controlling noise, structuring ATMIST, and making it easy for crews to transfer the information that changes the next five minutes.

dailyEditorial.title: Alternate Defibrillation Strategies for Refractory Ventricular Fibrillation
dailyEditorial.takeaway: If your service uses vector change or double sequential external defibrillation, this supports keeping that pathway rehearsed and deliberate. The gain will disappear quickly if it comes at the cost of longer pauses or confused choreography.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This DOSE-VF analysis reinforces the idea that refractory VF care may benefit from changing shock strategy instead of just repeating the same failed cycle.
dailyEditorial.summary: This DOSE-VF analysis reinforces the idea that refractory VF care may benefit from changing shock strategy instead of just repeating the same failed cycle. The interesting signal is mechanistic as well as clinical: less time spent in VF may be part of why alternative defibrillation approaches outperform standard repeats.

dailyEditorial.title: A Randomized Controlled Trial of a Digital Patient Decision Tool to Increase Sexually Transmitted Infection Testing in the Emergency Department
dailyEditorial.takeaway: If STI screening is inconsistent in your department, patient-directed tools are worth considering, but only if they slot cleanly into registration, consent, and follow-up pathways. Good implementation will matter more than the software itself.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: The STIckER trial is a workflow paper more than a diagnostics paper.
dailyEditorial.summary: The STIckER trial is a workflow paper more than a diagnostics paper. Its relevance is in showing that patient-facing digital decision support may increase STI testing in adolescents and young adults without relying entirely on clinician recall in a busy ED.

dailyEditorial.title: Endovascular Thrombectomy Boosts Outcomes in Medium-Vessel Ischemic Stroke
dailyEditorial.takeaway: Do not dismiss clinically important MeVO stroke as too distal to matter. Early stroke-team involvement remains reasonable when deficits are meaningful and treatment windows are still open.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: ORIENTAL-MeVO matters because medium-vessel occlusion has been a persistent gray zone in stroke systems.
dailyEditorial.summary: ORIENTAL-MeVO matters because medium-vessel occlusion has been a persistent gray zone in stroke systems. A signal toward better functional outcomes with thrombectomy is enough to keep these patients on the radar during triage, even if broader replication and local capability still shape what should happen next.

dailyEditorial.title: Association between prehospital time and trauma outcomes: A multicenter cohort study from the Pan-Asian Trauma Outcomes Study (PATOS)
dailyEditorial.takeaway: Use this to interrogate where delay accumulates in your trauma system rather than to chase one crude time target. The important question is which minutes are necessary care and which are pure system drag.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: PATOS adds detail to the old prehospital-time debate by showing that different components of delay may track with different outcomes.
dailyEditorial.summary: PATOS adds detail to the old prehospital-time debate by showing that different components of delay may track with different outcomes. It is helpful as a systems paper because it discourages simplistic use of total time alone while still making clear that prolonged rescue and scene intervals are not harmless.

dailyEditorial.title: Major adverse cardiac events in patients with indeterminate high-sensitivity troponin testing and chest pain: a systematic review
dailyEditorial.takeaway: A borderline troponin is not a discharge decision by itself. Re-anchor on serial change, clinical context, and pretest risk rather than forcing certainty from a single ambiguous number.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: Indeterminate high-sensitivity troponin results remain a common place where ED pathways overpromise clarity.
dailyEditorial.summary: Indeterminate high-sensitivity troponin results remain a common place where ED pathways overpromise clarity. This review highlights just how inconsistent downstream management still is, and how often serial testing is underused when a single value lands in the gray zone.

dailyEditorial.title: High-Risk Medication Prescribing Among Older Adults in the Emergency Department: A National Assessment
dailyEditorial.takeaway: Scrutinize discharge prescriptions in older adults with the same care you give admission medication review. Sedatives, anticholinergics, and other familiar high-risk choices still deserve a deliberate pause.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This national assessment gives a more concrete denominator to geriatric prescribing risk: nearly one in ten older adults filled a high-risk discharge medication within days of an ED visit.
dailyEditorial.summary: This national assessment gives a more concrete denominator to geriatric prescribing risk: nearly one in ten older adults filled a high-risk discharge medication within days of an ED visit. The improvement trend is encouraging, but the residual rate is high enough to make this a live patient-safety issue.

dailyEditorial.title: Association between initial bandemia degree and short-term mortality among adults with bacteremia in the emergency department
dailyEditorial.takeaway: Treat a high band count as another reason to widen your safety margin on disposition and reassessment. It should sharpen concern about severity, not replace the rest of the clinical picture.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: Bandemia is not a magic sepsis test, but this study argues it still carries prognostic weight in bacteremic adults.
dailyEditorial.summary: Bandemia is not a magic sepsis test, but this study argues it still carries prognostic weight in bacteremic adults. The useful point is not that band count dictates therapy; it is that marked bandemia should make clinicians less comfortable with seemingly middling presentations.

dailyEditorial.title: Prehospital prediction of clinical course in patients with suspected sepsis: a prospective cohort study
dailyEditorial.takeaway: Use prehospital sepsis scores to prepare attention and resources, not to close the diagnostic loop. Repeated bedside reassessment after ED arrival still matters more than any single prehospital risk label.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This prospective prehospital sepsis study is a useful check on score worship.
dailyEditorial.summary: This prospective prehospital sepsis study is a useful check on score worship. Ambulance-era variables and tools like qSOFA or NEWS2 carry some signal, but none cleanly predict shock, severity, and mortality all at once, which limits how far they can be pushed in isolation.
